---
title: "Climate"
description: "Canada's climate geography — how temperature and precipitation vary across the country"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
from pipeline.charts import point_value_map

MAP_CONFIG = {"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True,
              "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]}
norm = "data/geo/climate_normals.csv"
```

## How warm, how cold

Canada stretches across more than 40 degrees of latitude, and its climate varies
enormously from south to north and coast to interior. The map below shows the
**1981–2010 climate normals** — the 30-year averages used as the standard reference —
at roughly 660 weather stations. Use the dropdown to switch between **January**, **July**,
and the **annual** mean daily temperature. Blue is below freezing, red is above; hover any
station for its value, and zoom or pan to explore.

The contrast between the two seasons is the story: a January map that is almost entirely
blue — dipping below **−30 °C** across the North — and a July map where nearly the whole
country sits in comfortable double digits. That seasonal swing shapes how much energy
Canadians use to heat their homes, where and what can be farmed, and how infrastructure is
built.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(norm):
    df = pd.read_csv(norm)
    fig = point_value_map(df, "lat", "lon",
        groups=[("jan_temp", "January"), ("jul_temp", "July"), ("annual_temp", "Annual")],
        colorscale="RdBu", reversescale=True, cmid=0,
        cbar_title="Mean temp", value_suffix=" °C", name_col="station", zoom=2.3,
        source_note="Source: ECCC, Canadian Climate Normals 1981–2010 (station data).")
    fig.show(config=MAP_CONFIG)
else:
    print("Climate normals not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_geography`.")
```

## How wet, how dry

Precipitation is just as uneven. The **Pacific coast** is among the wettest places in the
country — parts of coastal British Columbia exceed **3,000 mm** a year — while the southern
**Prairies** and the **High Arctic** are genuinely dry, closer to **300 mm** or less. The
map shows annual total precipitation (rain plus the water equivalent of snow) at the same
stations.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(norm):
    dp = pd.read_csv(norm).dropna(subset=["annual_precip"])
    fig = point_value_map(dp, "lat", "lon",
        groups=[("annual_precip", "Annual precipitation")],
        colorscale="Blues", cmin=0, cbar_title="Precip", value_suffix=" mm",
        value_fmt=",.0f", name_col="station", zoom=2.3,
        source_note="Source: ECCC, Canadian Climate Normals 1981–2010 (station data).")
    fig.show(config=MAP_CONFIG)
```

## Download data

- [Climate normals by station (CSV)](../data/geo/climate_normals.csv) — ECCC Canadian Climate Normals 1981–2010 (mean temperature + precipitation)
